QF.id: Clubbing, Set-Off and Carry Forward of Losses_13

question.text: Mr. Girish has three minor children. The income from investments in their names is: Child 1 — Rs.8,000, Child 2 — Rs.12,000, Child 3 — Rs.5,000. Girish's income is Rs.15,00,000 and his wife's income is Rs.6,00,000. What is the total amount clubbed with Girish's income after exemption?

answer.text: Rs.25,000 — full income of all three children clubbed with Girish
Rs.20,500 — total Rs.25,000 minus Rs.1,500 exemption per child (Rs.4,500 total)
Rs.23,500 — total Rs.25,000 minus Rs.1,500 exemption for only one child
Rs.21,500 — total Rs.25,000 minus Rs.1,500 exemption for two children only (third child not eligible)

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Minor child income is clubbed with the parent having higher income — here Girish (Rs.15,00,000 > Rs.6,00,000). Total minor children's income = Rs.8,000 + Rs.12,000 + Rs.5,000 = Rs.25,000. Exemption under Section 10(32) is Rs.1,500 per child per year, for ALL children (not limited to two). Total exemption = Rs.1,500 x 3 = Rs.4,500. Amount clubbed = Rs.25,000 - Rs.4,500 = Rs.20,500. **The Trap:** Option D applies a "two children only" limit (confusing with Section 80C deduction for tuition fees which has no such restriction either, or with population policy concepts). The Rs.1,500 exemption applies per child with no limit on number of children. **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM Series X-A tests the per-child exemption calculation. The Rs.1,500 figure is a frequently tested number. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Rs.1,500 per child is a negligible exemption. The real planning insight is that minor child income clubbing can push parents into higher tax brackets — consider timing of investments carefully.
